source_metadata.title_raw: Joshua E. Morrison - A-18-010 - May 15, 2018 - Cerritos

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
, FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) :i:i:.t-S66o • Fax (916) a:z:i, 0886 
Joshua E. Morrison 
Atkinson, Andelson, Loya, Ruud & Romo 
12800 Center Court Drive, Suite 300 
Cerritos, CA 90703 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-010 
Dear Mr. Morrison: 
May 15, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Scott Bailey, Desert Sands 
Unified School District Superintendent, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of 
interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. Additionally, the Commission does not advise on past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b)(8).) Therefore, nothing in this advice letter should be construed to advise about any 
conduct that has already taken place. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are-required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Riverside County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. I ( c )(3 ). ) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal pr~ceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
. 
QUESTIONS 
(1) Would the waiver of conference fees and travel payments made to Superintendent Bailey by 
RTM Business Group ("RTM") be reportable where he participates in an advisory board to 
RTM? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the ~alifornia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-010 
Page No. 2 
(2) Would these payments preclude Superintendent Bailey from taking part in decisions 
regarding contracts between the Desert Sands Unified School District and private businesses 
that pay a fee to attend the same conferences ("R TM Clients") under the Act? 
(3) Would these payments preclude the District from contracting with RTM Clients under 
Section 1090, where Superintendent Bailey is considered an independent contractor to 
RTM, and the contract between the District and the RTM Client does not result in any 
compensation to RTM? 
CONCLUSIONS 
(1) The waiver of conference fees is not considered a gift or income under Regulation 18942 
("information material" exception). Other payments for travel including transportation, 
lodging, and meals provided as part of the conference are reportable as income so long as 
Superintendent Bailey provides equal or greater consideration for the travel. 2 
(2) No. Based on the facts provided, contracts between the District and RTM clients would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on RTM, and thus Superintendent 
Bailey would not be precluded from taking part in decisions related to such contracts. 
(3) No. Based on the facts provided, Superintendent Bailey does not have a prohibited financial 
interest in contracts between the District and RTM clients under Section 1090. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
Scott Bailey is the Superintendent for the Desert Sands Unified School District ("District") 
in Riverside County, and commenced his position in April 2017. In his previous employment as 
chief academic advisor to a Nevada school district, he participated as an ad hoc member of an 
advisory board to a private firm, RTM Business Group ("RTM"). Superintendent Bailey wishes to 
continue to serve on the RTM advisory board. 
RTM is a consultancy firm that organizes professional development conferences for 
education professionals. Public school administrators attend R TM conferences to collect 
information, collaborate, and share best practices regarding the implementation of technology in 
their districts. Those attending conferences, which are held across the continental United States, are 
required to pay a registration fee. 
Private companies also attend RTM conferences to market their goods and services to the 
education professionals in attendance ("RTM Clients"). RTM Clients must pay a fee to attend each 
conference. You indicate that, to your knowledge, RTM has no claim to any form of consideration 
arising from contracts between RTM Clients and educational institutions. 
2 You have indicated that past travel payments from RTM included coach-class airfare, a hotel room (not a 
suite), and meals also provided to all attendees. To the extent that future payments exceed those that have been 
previously provided, our analysis of whether you have provided equal or greater consideration may change, and you 
may want to seek further advice. 


File No. A-18-010 
Page No. 3 
RTM's informal ad hoc advisory board usually meets at the end of each RTM conference, 
with an RTM facilitator. The advisory board typically consists of 8-12 full-time employees of 
public school districts who attended the conference. You indicate that the goal of the meeting is to 
"provide feedback to RTM regarding the conference just conducted, to recommend topics and 
speakers for future conferences, and to ensure that content is always current and accurate." In 
exchange for their participation, RTM covers some or all of the public official's costs related to the 
conference, including conference fees, travel, lodging, and meals provided as part of the 
conference. 
Superintendent Bailey seeks to attend future RTM conferences (approximately 2 to 4) and 
serve on the advisory board following each conference he attends. He anticipates that RTM would 
then cover the cost of his travel, lodging, and conference fees. While you cannot provide us with an 
estimate of the payments Superintendent Bailey will receive in exchange for his participation on the 
Board, you advise that those payments will likely exceed $1,000 per year. You note that as a result 
of his participation on the advisory board, "RTM would pay for travel and conference expenses that 
would otherwise be paid by the District." 
ANALYSIS 
Conference Fees and Travel-Related Payments 
The Act requires public officials to report their sources of income - including gifts - for 
each calendar year on their Form 700 - Statement of Economic Interests ("Form 700"). (Section 
87202 and Section 87203.). The reporting threshold for sources of income differs from sources of 
gifts: $50 or more for gifts and $500 or more for income (not incJuding gifts). Gifts are limited to 
$470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) 
Section 82030 generally defines "income" as "a payment received, including but not limited 
to any salary, wage, advance, gift, reimbursement for expenses or per diem." A "gift," which is a 
form of"income," is defined, in relevant part, as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on 
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." (Section 
82028.) 
When a third party pays for a public official's travel, the payments are generally considered 
gifts to the public official. (Benninghoven Advice Letter, I-98-177.) Where an official provides 
consideration ( e.g., performed work or service) of equal or greater value for the payments, they are 
considered "income," but not "gifts." General guidelines to help determine whether consideration is 
equal or greater in value incJude: the length of time spent rendering the services, or whether the 
services are of the type not readily available from others. (Matulich Advice Letter, No. A-I-97-304.) 
Here, RTM plans to pay for Superintendent Bailey's travel, lodging, and meals and waive 
his conference fees in exchange for his participation on the advisory board at each conference he 
attends. As noted above, you have not provided an estimate of Superintendent Bailey's travel costs, 
but advise it will likely be greater than $1,000 per year. Because Superintendent Bailey has 
previously served on the advisory board and as a speaker at RTM conferences, his feedback would 
be particularly valuable to RTM. Tims, Superintendent Bailey's participation as an advisory board 


File No. A-18-010 
Page No. 4 
member at each conference would appear to serve as equal or greater consideration for the cost of 
his travel, lodging, meals and admission fee for each conference. Based on the facts provided, the 
travel payments would be considered income for reporting purposes, and not a gift. Please note that 
Regulations 18942 and 18942.1 provide an "informational material" exception for "free or 
discounted admission to informational conferences or seminars." Under this exception, the 
conference fee waiver is neither a "gift" nor "income" and thereby not required to be reported. 
Conflict of Interest - Political Reform Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official 
has a financial interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise include a source of 
income from which the official has received income of$500 or more within 12 months before the 
decision. (Section 87103(c).) You indicate that Superintendent Bailey will likely receive more than 
$1,000 per year in payments from RTM in exchange for his participation on the RTM advisory 
board. Based on the facts provided, Superintendent Bailey would have a financial interest in RTM 
as a source of income. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
An effect on a financial interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved 
in the decision. An interest is considered "explicitly involved" if it is a named party in, or subject 
of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) You indicate that RTM is not involved in contracts between 
RTM Clients and educational institutions. Under these facts, RTM would not be explicitly involved 
in District and RTM Client contract decisions. Therefore, the effect of a contract between a RTM 
Client and the District is only reasonably foreseeable if an effect on RTM can be recognized as "a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to an interest in a business 
entity not explicitly involved in a decision, including a source of income. 3 Regulation 18702.1 (b) 
provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is material "if a prudent person 
with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect would contribute to a change in the ... value of a privately-held business entity." 
You specify that to your knowledge RTM has no claim to any form of consideration arising 
from contracts between RTM Clients and educational institutions. RTM receives fees from RTM 
Clients for access to public education administrators at the RTM conferences. The current facts do 
not indicate that RTM's value is related to contractual relationships resulting from the access. 
Therefore, based on the facts provided, Superintendent Bailey's interest in RTM as a source of 
income would not generally preclude him from taking part in decisions related to contracts between 
the District and RTM Clients.4 Note, however, that this determination is necessarily fact specific 
3 See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4). 
4 We also find that the ''Nexus" rule provided in Regulation 18702.3(c) would not preclude Superintendent 
Bailey from taking part in decisions related to contracts between the District and private clients of R TM so long as 
RTM does not provide Superintendent Bailey with income for promoting contracts between the District and RTM 
Clients. Based ~n the facts provided, this is not the case, and thus the "Nexus" rule_ does not apply. 


File No. A-18-010 
Page No. 5 
and applicable only to RTM Clients that have paid to attend a RTM conference to market their 
goods and services to those in attendance. Any other business activity between RTM and a client 
may change the analysis. 
Government Code Section 1090 
You have also inquired as to whether Government Code Section I 090 would preclude future 
contracts between the District and RTM Clients, based on Superintendent Bailey's role as an 
independent contractor to RTM. Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract 
in which the o_fficial has a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in 
a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 
333.) Contracts found to be in violation of Section 1090 are void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
While Section 1090 does not define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney 
General Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may 
involve financial losses, or the possibility oflosses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (See 
e.g., Thomson, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 
867, fu. 5.) Past decisions state that the following economic relationships generally constitute a 
financial interest: employee, attorney, agent, or broker of a contracting party; supplier of services or 
goods to a contracting party; landlord or tenant of a contracting party; and, officer or employee of a 
nonprofit corporation that is a contracting party. 
Based on the facts provided, Superintendent Bailey will independently contract with RTM 
to provide very limited services of reviewing RTM conferences. He is neither an employee nor an 
owner of RTM and does not have the authority to act on its behalf. Additionally, you note that to 
the best of your knowledge neither Superintendent Bailey nor RTM would receive any direct or 
indirect compensation from a RTM Client contracting with any educational institution, including 
the District. Therefore, we find that RTM's payments to Superintendent Bailey for conference fees 
and travel would not generally create a prohibited financial interest, that would preclude the District 
from engaging in contracts with RTM Clients under the circumstances you have presented, or 
Superintendent Bailey from participating in decisions regarding the contracts. However, again we 
caution, that this determination is necessarily fact specific and applicable only to RTM Clients that 
have paid to attend a RTM conference to market their goods and services to those in attendance. 
Any other business activity between RTM and a client may change the analysis. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
AES:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
~~ 
By: 
Adam E. Silver 
Counsel, Legal Division

compensation to RTM?
